schools.summary: Matthew Henry reads Hezekiah's rent clothes and flight to the temple as a king wounded for God's honour, choosing prayer over a council of war.
Wesley echoes that great men must not think it a disparagement to sympathize with the injured honour of God; Clarke surveys the chapter's arc of distress, prayer, and deliverance.
Gill notes Hezekiah was moved by the report of Rabshakeh's speech from the preceding chapter.
Ellicott glosses the going up to the house of the LORD as humbling himself to pray for help (cf. 2 Chron. 32:20).
Keil notes the unusual word-order of Isaiah's name; Ellicott stresses that so distinguished an embassy, in sackcloth, marks the high honour in which the prophet stood.
Clarke sees Church and state uniting in fervent dependence on God through the prophet.
Keil and Ellicott parse the day of trouble, chastisement, and rejection, and the proverb of children come to the birth (cf. Hos. 13:13).
Clarke likens the state to a woman spent in travail, with a Homeric parallel; Wesley gives the proverb Judah's voice — a reformation begun but powerless to finish.
Keil takes 'hear' as noticing and punishing the blasphemy of the living God; Ellicott reads 'it may be' as hope not doubt, and the remnant as Zion left a hut in a vineyard (Isa. 1:8).
Wesley defines the remnant: Judah now the ten tribes are gone, Jerusalem now her fenced cities are taken.
Ellicott calls the verse a plain narrative resumption; Keil lays out Isaiah's comforting oracle and argues the 'report' is Tirhakah's advance (v.9), not the army's later destruction.

outliers.note: Corrects Thenius, who took the 'report' to be the news of the army's destruction in v.35; Keil argues Sennacherib was with the army and heard no such report.

themes: the report that turns him back

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott note the contemptuous 'servants' or 'squires' of the Assyrian king and that 'blasphemed' here is the harsher high-handed word (cf. Num. 15:30).
Ellicott reads the 'blast' as a spirit of despondency God breathes in; Keil ties the retreat to the report of v.9, hastened by the blow on the army.
Clarke identifies each clause fulfilled: the rumour of Tirhakah (v.9), the blast that slew 185,000 (v.35), and the sword of his sons (v.37).

debates.question: Is the 'blast/spirit' an inward impulse of fear or the outward pestilence?

sides.position: an inward spirit of despondency that makes him retreat
the outward blast, rumour, and sword fulfilled in the events

schools.summary: Henry sets the scene: Rabshakeh recalled to a king now besieging Libnah, hoping to frighten the once-pliant Hezekiah (2 Kings 18:14) into surrender.
Ellicott takes up the narrative from 2 Kings 18:37 and holds that Lachish was already taken (2 Chron. 32:9), the retreat under way.
Clarke places Libnah and Lachish near each other in the mountains of Judah; Wesley notes Rabshakeh returned to report the treaty.
Keil and Ellicott identify Tirhakah (Taharka), last king of the twenty-fifth Ethiopian dynasty, whose advance is the very 'report' of v.7 that provokes the second letter.
Keil paraphrases the taunt against Hezekiah's trust and the ban-verb of utter destruction; Ellicott notes 'deceive thee' targets every prophetic channel of comfort.
Clarke observes the letter merely repeats Rabshakeh's wall-speech (2 Kings 18:29).

themes: let not thy God deceive thee
the enemy has no new argument

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott expose 'destroyed utterly' as the ban — whole peoples devoted to extermination — the ground of Sennacherib's sneering 'shalt thou be delivered?'
Ellicott identifies Gozan, Haran, Rezeph, Bit-Adini, and Thelasar from the inscriptions; Keil takes 'them' back to 'all lands.'
Henry names the blasphemy: Sennacherib ranks the God of Israel among the beaten idols — 'and shall thy God deliver thee?'
Ellicott reads 'king' as a synonym for each city's local god (cf. Ps. 5:2); Keil carries Hezekiah up to spread the letter open before the LORD.

themes: where is the king of Hamath?
the letter spread before the LORD

schools.summary: Ellicott defends the act against Reuss's mockery: not to inform an ignorant God but to commit the whole cause solemnly into His hands.
Clarke reads the spreading of the letter as laying the blasphemy before God in His own dwelling, that He might read it.

themes: the letter spread before the LORD

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott expound the cherub-throned Creator as the one God of all kingdoms, the covenant relation and the argument from creation (Isa. 40:18).
Wesley confesses Jehovah the God of the whole earth, not of Israel only as Sennacherib fancied — universal and rightful Lord because Creator of all.

Keil hears the heaped imperatives as importunity and parses the plural 'eyes'; Ellicott insists 'bow thine ear, open thine eyes' means 'intervene actively,' attending to Sennacherib's words and letter.
Wesley notes the king would not honour Rabshakeh even by naming him.
Keil and Ellicott read 'of a truth' as a full concession of the Assyrian conquests — laid waste, perhaps under the ban — set up only to contrast the living God with the idols that fell.
Keil takes the words as Hezekiah's own ground of prayer — the idols were wood and stone; Ellicott airs and answers the critic's doubt whether such monotheism suited the age, citing Amos (Amos 5:8).

outliers.note: Raises the critical question whether the compiler put a stricter monotheism into Hezekiah's mouth than his age had reached, allowing the prayer may be a free composition, yet answering from Amos that the faith was already present.

themes: save us, that all may know

schools.summary: Henry calls it a gracious answer sealed by two immutable things (Heb. 6:18): the prayer against the oppressor is heard, for God hears the cry of the wronged.
Ellicott sets the scene — Isaiah relaying the revelation — and notes the verb 'I have heard' dropped from the parallel (Isa. 37:21).
Keil and Ellicott expound the personified virgin daughter of Zion, inviolable, shaking her head in scorn behind the retreating envoys (Ps. 22:8).
Clarke says even the young women of Jerusalem, under Jehovah, suffice to discomfit Assyria; Wesley reads 'virgin' of Zion's relative purity from idolatry, guarded like a daughter.

outliers.note: Recalls that a young woman of Gloucester took this verse as the motto of a pamphlet answering Bishop Warburton.

themes: the maiden laughs at the army

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott identify the object of the blasphemy — the Holy One of Israel — reading 'lifting the eyes on high' as looking heavenward, the arrogance lying in its object (cf. Isa. 40:26); the title bears Isaiah's own stamp.
Keil summarizes the boast of scaling Lebanon and felling its cedars; Ellicott reads the cedars and firs as figures for kings and nobles, Lebanon for the whole land (Isa. 2:13; Zech. 11:1).
Clarke likewise takes the cedars and firs for princes and nobles; Wesley keeps to the literal march of chariots over mountains thought inaccessible.

debates.question: Are the felled cedars and firs literal trees or figures of the nobility?

sides.position: figures for kings, princes, and nobles
the literal trees felled to clear the army's march

schools.summary: Clarke and Wesley read the strange waters and dried rivers as conquered lands and a host so vast its march drinks rivers dry.
Ellicott identifies the target as Lower Egypt — drying the Nile-arms of Mazor (Isa. 19:5) — the perfects perhaps a boasted future.
Keil and Ellicott set the divine purpose formed of old against the vaunt: the Assyrian only executed what God long before had fashioned (Isa. 10:5-15).
Clarke and Wesley press it home — 'this work is mine, not thine'; the conqueror is a mere instrument who could do nothing of his own might.

themes: I have done it long ago

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott gloss the conquered peoples as 'short-handed,' withering grass and blighted corn (Isa. 40:6; Ps. 129:6).
Wesley draws the nerve of 'therefore': they were of small power because God had armed Sennacherib and taken away their courage.
Keil reads God's knowledge of the king's every movement and rage; Ellicott notes a lost opening clause ('thine uprising is before me,' cf. Ps. 139:2).
Wesley: though thou knowest not me, I thoroughly know thee, thy secret contrivances and thy designs abroad.

themes: I know thy going out and coming in

schools.summary: Clarke pictures the hook and bridle as the gear for guiding a buffalo — the king led home like a hooked beast.
Ellicott grounds the threat in fact (Manasseh led with hooks, 2 Chron. 33:11) and in Keil's imagery of ringed lions and bridled horses.

themes: turned back the way he came

schools.summary: Clarke and Wesley expound the sign of the harvest — self-sown growth for two years, then sowing and reaping — proof the deliverance was no natural chance.
Ellicott calls it 'the sign' accrediting the greater word, glossing the after-growth and after-shoot (Lev. 25:5, 11).
Keil and Ellicott read the sign as a type of the escaped remnant striking firmer root, in Isaiah's own style (Isa. 6:13; 27:6).
Clarke reads the figure agriculturally — the thinned people multiplying like self-sown corn; Wesley: well fixed themselves, then doing good to others.
Henry lifts the image to the soul: rooted downward by faith in Christ, bearing fruit upward in righteousness.
Keil and Ellicott ground the promise in the zeal of the LORD of hosts — Isaiah's favourite remnant doctrine (Isa. 10:20-21; 37:32).
Clarke sees the remnant multiplying to fill Jerusalem and the land, some hearing the apostles' mission; Wesley: God will do it from zeal for His name and His undeserving people.

themes: the zeal of the LORD of hosts
the outcome as sure as God

schools.summary: Clarke tells off the mounting denials — no entry, no arrow, no army, no mound, not even a shield in the land; Wesley notes the army only blocked the city at a distance.
Ellicott and Keil read the fourfold refusal as the ordinary incidents of siege forbidden — arrow, shield, storming party, siege-bank (2 Sam. 20:15; Hab. 1:10).
Clarke distinguishes: the army shall not return, yet the king himself shall — reserved for a more ignominious death for his blasphemy.
Ellicott corrects the text to 'he came' (cf. v.28) and repeats the denial of v.32; Keil reads the Lord's word that he shall retire by his own road.
Ellicott renders 'I will cover this city with a shield' (Isa. 31:5) and refers 'for David's sake' to the covenant (2 Sam. 7); Keil reads the Lord's own undertaking to save.
Henry grounds the safety in God's own name and the sure mercies of David, fulfilled in Christ the Son of David.
Henry marvels at the swiftness — a destroying angel like the one that slew Egypt's firstborn (Isa. 31:8; Ps. 76:5), Josephus's pestilence leaving a field of corpses.
Clarke holds the angel was a suffocating pestilential wind, the 'blast' of v.7; Wesley names it such an angel as destroyed Egypt's firstborn.
Ellicott weighs the evidence for pestilence, holds a supernatural causation in any case, and (with Herodotus) locates the camp in Egypt, not before Jerusalem.

debates.question: By what means did the angel of the LORD smite the 185,000?

sides.position: a personal destroying angel, as slew Egypt's firstborn
a natural agent — a pestilence or suffocating wind — as God's instrument

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott hear the hurried heaping of verbs — decamped, marched, returned, remained — and note he never again invaded the west.
Clarke marks Nineveh the capital; Wesley reads the crowded clauses as the king's disorder and distraction of mind.
Keil identifies Nisroch as an eagle-deity; Ellicott works the corrupt name and the fugitive sons (Adrammelech, Nergal-sar-usur) fleeing to Ararat, Esarhaddon succeeding (681-668 B.C.).
Clarke reports the rabbinic tale of a threatened sacrifice; Wesley draws the moral that his blood is justly mingled with his sacrifices.
Henry weighs the justice: sons rebel against the father as he rebelled against God; Esarhaddon later colonized Samaria (2 Kings 17:24; Ezra 4:2).

debates.question: Why did the sons murder Sennacherib at his worship?

sides.position: to forestall being sacrificed by him to his god (rabbinic tradition)
a righteous judgment — parricide answering his rebellion against God
